metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `_engine` (reconciler subprocess + package data) should be included in the Phase 2 per-package `disallow_untyped_defs` candidate list, or excluded with a note that it is package-data code outside the type-checked import surface.
findings.finding: The plan lists `_engine` as a candidate package for empirical `disallow_untyped_defs` scoping, but `src/rebar/_engine/` is not a normal importable Python sub-package in the way the other 12 candidates are: it ships as package data containing the `rebar_reconciler` subprocess and `jira-capability-probe.py` script. With `follow_imports = silent`, mypy only visits files explicitly named on the command line (`mypy src/rebar`); whether `_engine` contents are actually type-checked (and thus whether they would generate new errors under `disallow_untyped_defs`) is non-obvious and not addressed by the plan. If the plan's Phase 2 empirical test silently skips `_engine` contents, the package could end up in the 'clean' set by default rather than by merit.
findings.location: Phase 2 — per-package `disallow_untyped_defs` / package list in What/files
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `_engine` from the Phase 2 candidate package list and add a comment in the plan (and later in pyproject) noting that `src/rebar/_engine/` ships as package data and is not part of the mypy-gated import surface. If the reconciler IS visited by mypy, document why and test it separately.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-count `type: ignore` occurrences in `src/rebar/` (e.g. `grep -r 'type: ignore' src/rebar/ | wc -l`) and update the '≈48' figure in the Why section; if the true count materially exceeds 48, revise the Phase 1 scope estimate and the 'ratchet is cheap' rationale accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '≈48 type: ignore' across src/rebar, but a grep of src/rebar for 'type: ignore' returns more than 48 hits before the tool's truncation limit, meaning the actual count in production source is materially higher. This premise directly supports 'the ratchet is cheap' and implicitly predicts the Phase 1 fix-up burden — if the real count is significantly larger, the estimate is wrong and the 'minimal, code-tagged annotations' remediation scope understates the work.
findings.location: Why section — 'In-practice discipline is decent (≈48 `type: ignore`, ≈204 `Any` across 77k LOC)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `grep -r 'type: ignore' src/rebar | wc -l` to get the actual count, then either correct the figure in the plan or narrow the claim to 'src/rebar (excluding _engine/)' if that produces ~48 and the plan's intent was the core packages only.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the guard implementation form explicitly: confirm whether the exempt-list guard follows the module-size pattern (Python unit test in `tests/unit/` + optional shell gate in `test.yml`) rather than a standalone shell grep, so the enforcement style stays consistent with the codebase's established ratchet convention.
findings.finding: The exempt-list guard is specified as 'CI grep or a short check step' without resolving which form to use, risking a new ad-hoc shell step when the codebase's established pattern for ratchet guards is a Python unit test (test_module_size_contract.py) plus a shell gate — adding a third, non-aligned enforcement form would mildly proliferate config-enforcement surface.
findings.location: Phase 2 / Guard section: 'a CI grep (or a short check step) that fails the build if the exempt list grows beyond its committed contents, mirroring the module-size allowlist pattern'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'implement the exempt-list guard as a Python unit test (e.g. `tests/unit/test_mypy_exempt_contract.py`) that asserts the `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]` modules-without-`disallow_untyped_defs` set matches a committed reference list, mirroring `test_module_size_contract.py`'s structure — not a new shell-grep step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to one specific guard mechanism (e.g., a bash diff of the `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]` exempt-module list against a committed `.github/mypy-exempt-packages.txt` file, mirroring `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` exactly) rather than leaving 'CI grep or check step' as an open either/or.
findings.finding: The guard mechanism is specified as 'a CI grep (or a short check step)' — two structurally different implementations without committing to one. An implementer cannot determine which artifact to create (a grep in the CI YAML vs. a Python/shell check script), what it should verify (line count of exempt entries, or membership of the override list), or how it maps to the existing module-size-allowlist pattern the plan explicitly invokes as a precedent.
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the module-size-allowlist pattern exactly: commit a `.github/mypy-exempt-packages.txt` (one dotted module pattern per line), reference it from a CI step that diffs the committed file against the `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]` modules list parsed from pyproject.toml, and fail if the live override list has entries not in the committed file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default guard implementation in the plan (e.g., 'prefer a `grep -c` one-liner in CI; fall back to a short Python script only if the TOML structure makes the grep fragile') so the executor has a concrete default to act on.
findings.finding: The guard mechanism in Phase 2 and AC3 is stated as 'CI grep or a short check step' — two options without committing to one. An executor cannot act on an unresolved either/or; this is a defaultable gap the plan should resolve by naming the default approach.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Guard bullet: 'Default: a `grep` one-liner in the CI step that counts the packages in the exempt override block and fails if the count exceeds the committed baseline N; use a short Python/shell script only if pyproject.toml structure makes a reliable grep impractical.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note in the plan body (e.g. under Phase 1 or Scope) confirming that `make test` is a pass-through safety net requiring no dedicated work, so AC5 has an explicit corresponding description.
findings.finding: AC5 (`make test` green) is an orphan criterion with no corresponding work described in the plan body; the plan body only ever mentions keeping `make typecheck` green, never `make test`.
findings.suggested_fix: Under Phase 1, add: 'No test changes are expected; `make test` is verified green as a safety net to confirm annotation fixes are non-behavioral.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a red-path proving step for the guard: describe a command (e.g., temporarily add a fake package to the exempt block and run the guard check, assert it exits non-zero, then revert) so AC3 can be verified end-to-end.
findings.finding: AC3 ('a guard prevents the exempt list from growing') has no proving command that demonstrates the guard actually fires — i.e., that CI truly fails when a package is added to the exempt list. The plan describes the guard's existence but not how to verify it enforces the ratchet.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Guard AC: 'Proof: in a throwaway commit, append a dummy entry to the exempt override block and confirm the guard step exits non-zero; revert before merging.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
